MANAGER COMMENTARY

As of 12/31/2024

“Discussions surrounding the administration’s potential trade policies raised questions about the direction of tariffs, particularly with key global trading partners.”

EARNEST Partners

Market in Review

Global equity markets were mixed during the fourth quarter of 2024, as macroeconomic and geopolitical factors weighed on investor sentiment. U.S. equities saw moderate gains, with the S&P 500 rising by 2.40%. Mid-cap stocks, represented by the Russell Midcap® Index, posted a small gain of 0.62%. International markets faced challenges, as the MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. returned -7.60% and the MSCI EAFE was down 8.11. Fixed-income markets also struggled, as the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index declined by 3.06%, reflecting rising yields and persistent inflation concerns.

The U.S. economy showed continued strength during this period. With a new U.S. presidential administration on the horizon, investors turned their attention to potential changes in tariffs, tax policies, and government spending. Discussions surrounding the administration’s potential trade policies raised questions about the direction of tariffs, particularly with key global trading partners. At the same time, expectations of tax reforms and infrastructure spending fueled speculation about their economic impact. The Federal Reserve (“Fed”) enacted two 0.25% rate cuts during the quarter, one in November and another in December. With these adjustments, the fed funds rate now stands in the range of 4.25% to 4.50%. The Fed signaled that it would reduce rates further in 2025, but not as much as it previously expected, reflecting the central bank’s continued concern with inflation even as the Consumer Price Index remained below 3.0%.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter of 2024, the Harbor Small Cap Value Fund (Institutional Class, “Fund”) returned -0.64%, outperforming its benchmark, the Russell 2000® Value Index, which returned -1.06%.

Contributors & Detractors

Archrock (AROC) is a leading provider of natural gas compression services in the United States. The company’s equipment and services are essential for moving natural gas from production sites to pipelines, ensuring efficient transportation. Its solutions play a critical role in the energy supply chain, supporting the reliability and functionality of natural gas infrastructure. In the most recent quarter, Archrock delivered strong financial results, with a 10% year-over-year increase in revenue, driven by robust demand for its compression services amid rising natural gas production. The company also reported a 7% improvement in adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization), reflecting operational efficiencies and favorable pricing trends in its service contracts. Archrock's ability to expand its fleet utilization rates and secure long-term contracts further contributed to performance. The company’s dominant position in natural gas compression and its focus on expanding its service offerings make it a compelling investment well positioned for sustained growth.

Stifel Financial (SF) is a diversified financial services company providing investment banking, wealth management, and brokerage services. The firm serves a wide range of clients, including individuals, institutions, and corporations, with tailored financial solutions. Stifel’s strong reputation and expansive advisory network make it a key player in the financial services industry. In the most recent quarter, Stifel reported a 12% increase in investment banking revenue, driven by strong activity in mergers and acquisitions and equity capital markets. The wealth management segment also posted solid growth, with assets under management rising 8% year over year due to positive market performance and net new client inflows. The company’s focus on expanding its advisory capabilities and strategic hiring initiatives contributed to its strong results. Stifel’s leadership in investment banking and wealth management, coupled with its diversified business model, make it a compelling investment.

Buys & Sells

No positions were added during the quarter. We exited one position in United Fire Group (UFCS). United Fire provides property and casualty insurance products, including personal, commercial, and specialty insurance, primarily across the United States. We sold the stock due to crowding out.

Sector Underweights & Overweights

Earnest Partners does not target sector or industry weights. Instead, the weightings are an outgrowth of our bottom-up, fundamental stock selection process. Our process is designed to put the client in the individual securities that we believe have attractive expected returns — relative overweights and underweights are an outgrowth of where we are finding those individual opportunities. The risk management process also influences the weights taken in any one sector. Generally, larger sectors will not represent more than the greater of 35% or 2.5x the benchmark weight.

Outlook

As bottom-up fundamental investors who aim to exceed performance over a full market cycle, we do not maintain an economic forecast. However, we have found the most opportunities within the Industrials, Financials, and Information Technology sectors.

data.title: Bitcoin Surrenders $80000 Support Amid Tariff Induced Volatility, Capital Inflows Grind To A Halt
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Bitcoin prices were not spared when President Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs set shockwaves through global markets. The policy has been put on hold for a majority of countries, but the ongoing China-US stalemate has kept volatility high and market participants on edge.

Bitcoin rose around 6.6% on Wednesday to trade back above the psychological $80000 handle as President Trump announced a pause to tariffs. The announcement was a 90-day pause on new tariffs for more than 75 countries, except China, has been announced. Investors saw this as a hopeful sign that the tariff increases might slow down for now. However, President Trump raised tariffs on China to 125%, showing that the trade fight between the two largest economies is far from over.

The question is whether this is another false dawn and new lower high or are we in for another bullish rally? Let us take a look at a few signs that may shed some light on Bitcoin's next potential move.

What do historical bear markets tell us?

Many market participants cheered the recent rebound, but experts like those at Goldman Sachs warn that bear market rallies are common and can be deceptive.

Goldman's "Bear Market Anatomy" report shows there have been 19 such rallies since the 1980s, usually lasting 44 days with 10-15% gains, but often followed by bigger drops. Callum Thomas from Topdown Charts shared a similar view, noting that the 1930s bear market had several big rallies before it finally hit bottom. "Is this 90-day recovery just another bear market rally?" he questioned.

An interesting take and something that market participants need to remain weary off. Until countries begin to sign off on trade deals and we have some movement on the US-China situation my take is that markets are not out of the woods yet.

Capital inflows have ground to a halt

Since the beginning of the year, less capital has been flowing into both Bitcoin and Ethereum. This is shown by the 30-day Realized Cap change, which tracks monthly changes in the total money moving in and out of these assets.

Bitcoin once had $100 billion flowing in monthly, but now that's down to just $6 billion per month. Ethereum, which peaked at $15.5 billion per month, is now losing $6 billion monthly.

For Bitcoin, the slowing inflows suggest fewer new investments are coming in to push prices higher. Meanwhile, Ethereum is seeing money spent at lower prices than it was bought for, leading to a net loss. This makes Ethereum face greater challenges than Bitcoin, which is also reflected in Ethereum's weaker price performance.

Just like the bear market warning above, the decline in capital flows is significant and a warning sign to bulls that the bearish pressure may not be done just yet.

Looking at ETF flows as well and one can see from the table below that ETFs have struggled since the liberation day tariff announcement. However, if we are being honest, ETF flows have been poor since early February with brief spurts of positive flows followed by significant declines.

Since April 2, the Bitcoin ETFs have seen 5 consecutive days of outflows with the highest amount occurring on Tuesday April 8, with a print of $326.3 million.

Glassnode hints that seller exhaustion may be starting to develop. What does it mean?

According to research from Glassnode, after the recent sharp market drop, it’s important to look at how investors are reacting, as bear markets often begin with fear and big losses.

By analyzing Bitcoin's realized losses over 6-hour periods, we can get a clearer picture of investor behavior during this downturn.

At its peak, $240 million in losses were recorded in just six hours, matching some of the biggest loss events in this cycle.

However, with each price drop, the amount of losses has gotten smaller. This may indicate that sellers are running out of steam for now at this price level.

The amount of losses is getting smaller with each price drop, which could mean investors are getting used to the lower prices and volatile market conditions. This does not necessarily point to a rally for Bitcoin but rather shows that market participants are expecting such conditions to linger for a while longer.

Technical Analysis - BTC/USD

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) from a technical standpoint on the daily timeframe remains in a bearish trend. This despite yesterday's significant bullish engulfing daily candle close.

The recent rise on Wednesday of around 6% failed to take out the previous lower high at 84000 and leaves the world's largest crypto susceptible to further downside.

As you can see from today's daily candle which is down 4.26% at the time of writing, the bears are not done yet.

The RSI period-14 rejected the neutral 50 level today, another sign that bearish momentum remains very much in play.

Given the dynamics discussed above, a further drop in Bitcoin prices remains on the table with immediate support resting at 78197 and 76000 before the psychological 75000 handle comes back into focus. Below that we have the 73777, 71935 and 70000 handles to monitor.

A recovery from here needs to record a daily candle close above the 84000 handle for bulls to fully take charge. Beyond that we have a crucial confluence level around 85000 where the descending trendline and the 50-day MA rest. For now, this seems like a tough hurdle to overcome and we may need to see some change to market dynamics.

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) Daily Chart, April 10, 2025

Support

  • 76000
  • 75000
  • 73777

Resistance

  • 80000
  • 84000
  • 85000 (key confluence level)

data.title: Sentiment Stays Bearish
data.description:
Even before the extreme volatility of the past week, the technical correction in stock prices had meant that investor sentiment tanked. Granted, even when the S&P 500 was last at an all-time high in mid-February sentiment leaned bearish with the percentage of respondents to the weekly AAII survey reporting as bulls only sitting at 29.2% as of February 20. Over the next three weeks, it dropped to local lows in the 19% range and as of today's release, it was back up to 28.5%. In other words, even through all the crazy moves in the market, investors amazingly appear to be only slightly less bullish than they were at the time of the February 19 high.

Of course, that is still a muted level of bullish sentiment. Meanwhile, the percentage of respondents reporting as bears is much more elevated than it was two months ago. Whereas the February 20 reading in bears was only 40.5%, today it is at 58.9% which was down versus 61.9% last week.

That means that investors continue to overwhelmingly report bearish sentiment with increased polarization to boot. The share of respondents reporting neutral sentiment reaffirms this. That share dropped to a meager 12.5% in the latest week's data. That ranks in the first percentile of readings in the full history of the data dating back to 1987 and is the lowest reading since it came in at 11% on May 28, 2009.

Of course, other sentiment surveys offer additional looks at investor sentiment. One such report is the Investors Intelligence survey which has a survey base of newsletter writers. One perk of this survey is a much longer history beginning back in 1963. This week's release indicated the lowest level of bullish sentiment, 23.6%, since December 2008. Before that, the last time sentiment was this weak was in July 1994. So it's been rare for investors to be this outright negative of equities.

We would also note that this survey collects data through Tuesday afternoons with a release early Wednesday mornings. That means this latest data would not have reflected any reaction to yesterday's update on tariffs nor the massive surge in stock prices in response.

That weak sentiment is unsurprising given the trade war's implications for the global economy and the collapse and general volatility in stock prices we have seen in the past week. To help further quantify this, in the chart below we show the percentage spread between the S&P 500's closing highs and lows for all one-week periods ending Tuesday, which coincides with the Investors Intelligence survey's collection period, going back to the start of the survey data in 1963. In the latest week, we saw a 12% range between the S&P 500's high and low on a closing basis, one of the more volatile weeks of this period.

In the chart below, we show those weeks when the S&P 500 had a 10%+ range between high and low closing prices during the Investors Intelligence survey collection period while also trading lower during the span. As shown, the past week was the 18th example. For starters, the S&P 500's range this go around was middling (47th percentile) for these occurrences although the decline was one of the larger ones, slightly outpacing the median decline of 9.5%.

As for the changes in sentiment, bullish sentiment according to the Investors Intelligence survey is the second lowest of these instances behind late October 2008. The week-over-week decline was also larger than normal, nearly doubling the average move. While that survey's bearish sentiment reading wasn't even in line with the average, the week-over-week uptick was again more than double what has historically been the norm during weeks with this much volatility. In other words, currently, we are seeing extreme volatility and extremely bearish investor sentiment, especially among investment professionals (i.e. newsletter writers).

data.title: CarMax: Growth Mode, Buy The Drop
data.description:
In today's column, we discuss the just reported earnings and outlook for CarMax, Inc. (NYSE:KMX). This is a stock we have traded many times, but it is getting crushed now. However, we continue to hold a long-term house position.

Overall, there were strengths and weaknesses in the quarter, but the company came up well short of expectations. While inflation in car pricing is coming down, and we saw lower car prices, we still are in a period of high interest rates and a consumer who is just under so much pressure. Now tariffs are here, which will add more pressure to consumer's wallets. But should you buy this huge decline in KMX stock? Let's examine the performance here.

CarMax's Q4 2025 Revenues Rise

The top line was positive here. CarMax's just reported revenues were a sizable increase from last year. We saw a mixed picture of volumes and pricing. Combined sales volume was up 4.9% versus last year's Q4. Q4 revenues were $6.0 billion, but this was a $30 million beat versus consensus, per the Seeking Alpha release summary. Now, over the last few quarters, we have seen used car pricing normalize some, although tariffs on new cars are thought to bolster the used car market. Here in fiscal Q4, there was a slight increase in the used car average selling prices, and a minimal increase in wholesale prices which were positive.

CarMax Q4 2025 Comparable Sales

Comparable sales are the critical indicator we watch for CarMax. Total retail used vehicle unit sales rose 6.2% in the quarter, and comparable store used unit sales also increased 5.1% versus the prior year's Q4. As noted, volume was up of course, but used car prices were up 0.6%. Bill Nash, President, and CEO of CarMax stated in the press release:

We are pleased with the continuing momentum across our diversified business during the fourth quarter. We delivered robust EPS growth driven by increases in unit sales and buys, strong growth in total gross profit, an increase in CAF income, and ongoing management of SG&A. During fiscal year 2025, we further differentiated our consumer offering and drove incremental operational efficiencies...providing a strong runway for future growth.”

While the earnings were a miss, it was a good quarter. The stock is tanking. We think you can get a nice bounce off this action.

That said, total retail used vehicle revenues increased 7.5% compared to last year, driven by the higher volume and the slight increase in average retail selling price. Total wholesale vehicle unit sales rose 3.1%, while total wholesale revenues were up 3.5% as the average wholesale selling price ticked up 0.1%. The so-called “other sales and revenues” increased by 2.7% or $4.2 million from last year. The increase stemmed from increased sales of extended protection plan revenues and better margins.

CarMax Q4 2025 Margins and Profits

Higher volumes and higher selling prices combined led to better profits on the retail side of the business, but were down in the wholesale business. Total gross profit increased to $667.9 million, up 10.6% from last year. On the retail side, used vehicle gross profit increased by 9.5%. Retail gross profit per used unit was up $71 from last year to $2,322. Over on the wholesale side of the business, gross profit was up actually down 3.8% from last year because the cost to acquire vehicles was up, leading to the gross profit per unit decreasing by $75 from a year ago to $1,045.

That said, with an increase in the top line but higher SG&A expenses and capex, earnings were $0.58 per share, missing consensus by $0.08 per share. Still, this was a huge increase from $0.32 last year. So the growth is there, folks. We also want to point out that in Q4, CarMax repurchased 1.2 million shares of common stock for $98.5 million, accelerating its pace of repurchases. At the end of the quarter, it had $1.94 billion remaining available for repurchase on its authorization.

Looking Ahead to Fiscal 2025 Expectations and A Word On Tariffs

For fiscal 2026, the company is targeting six new stores, and four standalone reconditioning/auction facilities. The company is still working toward a long-term target of 2 million units sold annually. For fiscal 2026, capex was higher than expected, guided at $575 million stemming from land purchase timing. The company also indicated its model is for ongoing double-digit earnings growth.

As for tariffs, it is widely believed that this will increase used car prices higher. And on the conference call Q+A, we did hear from Bill Nash that they are seeing higher demand already in the franchise dealers. However, they acknowledged where they will be impacted is on the parts side of the business where they will spend more to recondition cars. The company thinks it can offset these from operational efficiencies.

For fiscal 2026 it is hard to project with what is going on right now in the world, but we think we see growth. For this fiscal year, we are targeting revenue growth of 2-6%, on the thought that prices will be up some, while volume grows mid-single digits. On the EPS front, not counting any share repurchases, we think you see $3.70 to $4.20. This would be growth of significantly from $3.21 in fiscal 2025. As such, we think this decline is a buy based on the earnings growth.

We are looking for sales that are flat to up 3%, with margins commensurate with fiscal 2024. With this result, we think EPS can head toward $3.20-$3.60 for the year, valuing shares at 24.4X FWD EPS at $83 per share. We think this is a rather fair value for the results expected. While we provided you with another rapid-return trade here, we think you can leave some profit in the long term, and if we retrace again, consider upping the position, provided the operational outlook remains positive.

data.title: 3 Themes Driving America's Manufacturing Revival
data.description:
For decades, U.S. companies prioritized offshore manufacturing, optimizing for lower costs and efficiency through global supply chains. Now, a convergence of economic and geopolitical forces is driving a reversal—manufacturing is coming back home. This transformation does not appear to be just a short-term economic shift, but a strategic recalibration of national priorities to rebuild industrial capacity, strengthen supply chain resilience, and secure long-term competitiveness.

Reshoring, however, will require more than just favorable policy. Large investments in U.S. infrastructure, automation, and energy supply will be essential to transform the U.S. into a modern-era industrial powerhouse. The Global X U.S. Infrastructure Development ETF (PAVE), the Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF (BOTZ), and the Global X U.S. Electrification ETF (ZAP) could be potential strategies investors can leverage to get exposure to these themes.

Key Takeaways

  • U.S. Infrastructure: Construction activity is expected to remain elevated, due to the modernization of roads, ports, and logistics networks that will likely be needed to sustain domestic production activity, alongside the build out of new manufacturing facilities through private investments.
  • Industrial Robotics: Hyper-automation will be critical to mitigate labor shortages and drive productivity while maintaining cost competitiveness.
  • U.S. Electrification: Rising manufacturing activity and its power-intensive nature is expected to increase electricity demand, requiring power grid upgrades to support reshoring-driven energy needs.

U.S. Infrastructure: Laying the Foundation for a Production Boom

In our view, manufacturing revival in the U.S. hinges on domestic infrastructure improving at a pace that matches the rise of production activity. We’re not just talking about a one-off upgrade, but a sustained, multi-year transformation—one that includes not only the construction of new factories but also the modernization of critical infrastructure to support a domestic production ecosystem.

A historical parallel can be drawn to China’s infrastructure boom in the 2000s, which played a pivotal role in its manufacturing dominance. Between 2002 and 2016, China’s infrastructure investment as a share of GDP surged from 8% to 24%, enabling its rapid industrial expansion.1 While the U.S. benefits from a strong infrastructure foundation built over the 20th century, maintaining momentum in reshoring manufacturing will require significant infrastructure investment to meet the demands of a revitalized industrial base.

That momentum is already building. In the wake of the pandemic, U.S. manufacturers have aggressively increased domestic facility construction. In 2024, U.S. manufacturing-related construction spending surged to $236 billion—more than doubling from end of 2021 levels.2 A sustained pipeline of projects remains in planning phases, reinforcing the trend.3

Federal trade policy is adding further pressure. The use of tariffs, particularly under the Trump administration, has accelerated the shift toward domestic manufacturing. In the last few months, we’ve seen new reshoring investments, including massive commitments from companies such as Apple and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC), potentially adding up to hundreds of billions in infrastructure spending.4,5

The challenge, however, extends beyond building new facilities. American infrastructure has generally been in a poor state. A newly released study by the American Society of Civil Engineers (ASCE) graded U.S. infrastructure a C. The grade, although a modest improvement from a C- allotted in 2021, points to aging roads, deteriorating bridges, and outdated water systems, alongside broader issues in energy, food supply, and other infrastructure sectors.6 The U.S. transportation network was designed for an era of offshored manufacturing, not for a rebirth of domestic production. Without modernized roads, rail networks, and ports, logistics bottlenecks could derail the reshoring trend. In all, the ASCE estimates that $9.1 trillion in infrastructure investments will be needed over the next decade.

The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) of 2021 addresses this challenge of modernizing U.S. infrastructure, allocating nearly $1.2 trillion to upgrade transportation, energy, and broadband infrastructure. Although the legislation has come under political crossfire, in our view, it’s likely safe from significant cutback or full repeal given the central role it plays in strengthening the U.S. infrastructure base at such a pivotal moment.

Industrial Robotics: No Reshoring Without Hyper-Automation

While automation is often seen as a threat to jobs, it is likely the linchpin of America’s manufacturing revival. Although rising wages abroad and the growing risks of global supply chains have narrowed the cost gap between U.S. and offshore production, automation can provide a definitive competitive edge that makes reshoring not just feasible but imperative.

For manufacturers, automation is the solution to two key challenges—labor shortages and cost parity. For sectors like semiconductors, EV batteries, and aerospace—first in line to reshore considering their critical importance—automation is not only cost-effective but essential for achieving the high-precision and throughput required. These sectors demand exacting production standards that only state-of-the-art robotic systems can consistently meet.

The economic case for robotics is also strengthening. The cost of deploying industrial robots is estimated to have declined nearly 25% in the last decade due to advances in sensors, software, and hardware.7 Meanwhile, U.S. manufacturing wages rose nearly 4% in 2024 and are projected to climb another 35% by 2030, further supporting automation’s role in maintaining cost competitiveness.8

This shift is already well underway. As of 2023, global manufacturers operated nearly 4.3 million industrial robots, marking 10% year-over-year (YoY) growth.9 The U.S. market has seen a surge in adoption, with manufacturers installing 44,303 robots in 2023, a 12% increase from the prior year.10 Canada saw even stronger growth, with installations rising 43% YoY, driven by the automotive sector.11

Looking ahead, advancements in AI, edge computing, and sensors should continue to redefine industrial automation. Robotics is evolving from fixed, pre-programmed machines into adaptive, intelligent systems capable of handling complex, dynamic tasks. Future industrial robots, including humanoids, could work seamlessly alongside human operators, taking on dangerous or repetitive tasks while allowing workers to focus on higher-value functions.

U.S. Electrification: Manufacturing Amplifies Power Needs

The expansion of U.S. manufacturing is inextricably tied to the country’s ability to meet rising electricity demand. History offers some evidence. In 1900, manufacturing accounted for roughly 10% of U.S. electricity consumption, rising to well over 50% by 1950, driven by electrified assembly lines and industrial processes.12 The post-WWII boom accelerated this trend, with industrial electricity consumption growing at roughly 7% annually from 1945 to 1970.13

This power-growth dynamic could reemerge in the coming decades, as the U.S. reshapes manufacturing, especially in sectors like semiconductors, EVs, and solar energy. A semiconductor fabrication facility (fabs), for example, can use as much power as 30,000 homes, highlighting the power-intensive nature of the industry.14 Nearly seventy-five semiconductor fabs are under construction in the U.S.15

Emerging semiconductor processes are also becoming more energy hungry. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC), for example, is expected to consume nearly 24% of Taiwan’s electricity by 2030 as it dominates high-end chip manufacturing—those under 10 nanometers (nm).16 The U.S. is projected to produce nearly 20% of such high-end semiconductors by 2030, starting from zero in 2022.17

As of 2022, industrial sector use makes up about 35% of total U.S. electricity demand, according to the EIA.18 And as electrification increases—across industries, data centers, and electrified vehicle fleets—U.S. electricity demand is projected to grow nearly 47% by 2040 which, alongside power generation, underscores the need for grid modernization.19,20

The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) has allocated $394 billion to clean energy initiatives, accelerating investments in renewable energy, grid resilience, and battery storage, looking to spur the power generation sector.21 But more will likely be needed. As more factories are built domestically, particularly in the industrial heartlands, the U.S. will need to expand both power generation capacity and transmission infrastructure to support these energy-intensive industries.

Conclusion: U.S. Reindustrialization Presents an Attractive Opportunity

The reshoring of U.S. manufacturing is unlikely a fleeting trend—it appears to be a generational transformation that will require sustained investment in infrastructure, automation, and electrification. The opportunities for investors could be significant, as these structural shifts will likely drive demand across multiple sectors. This realignment of national priorities could likely reshape America’s economic landscape and could secure its competitive edge for decades to come. We believe pioneering companies, across the three themes we highlight, are likely to benefit from these strengthening tailwinds.

Related ETFs

PAVE – Global X U.S. Infrastructure Development ETF

BOTZ – Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF

ZAP – Global X U.S. Electrification ETF

Footnotes

data.title: Luxfer Holdings: Its Operations May Be Affected By The Blanket Tariffs
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to take a look at Luxfer Holdings PLC’s (NYSE:LXFR) performance in 2024 and give some comments on its outlook regarding tariffs and the long-lasting sale of its unprofitable Graphics Arts segment.

The last time I covered the stock was before its Q3 earnings. Since then, LXFR shares have plummeted around 22%, and I don’t see this trend reversing anytime soon. I maintain my Hold rating.

Briefly on Performance

Starting from the top, the company’s sales took a slight hit in the middle of the year, and by the end of the year, it more or less recovered, which means there wasn’t much growth overall. Challenges persisted within the clean energy markets as well as the compressed natural gas truck segment. Additionally, a slowdown in hydrogen adoption in Europe impacted the company’s performance.

Now, onto the company’s efficiency and profitability, we can see that LXFR’s margins across the board improved quite decently, even as sales weren’t particularly impressive. The company’s strategic consolidation of some U.S. operations helped boost its profitability, especially in the alternative fuels and powder business. So, operationally at least, the company is continuing to streamline in the right direction and that is encouraging.

On to the other efficiency and profitability metrics, since we saw the company’s bottom line improve significantly from Q2 onwards, it’s no surprise that the company’s ROA and ROE have seen a similar improvement. These are going in the right direction; however, they are still not too appealing from an efficiency perspective. The management is heading in the right direction, and I can see these improving further with more streamlining efforts of operations, but there’s still a lot of work left.

Also, the company’s return on total capital as compared to its peers (selected by Seeking Alpha by default), is much higher but is still below my minimum of 10%. However, seeing that none of the companies can achieve an ROTC above 10%, it may be a sector-wide issue, and I shouldn’t expect a lot from it. Although Luxfer managed to reach it back in 2021, this tells me the company has lost some of its competitive edge.

Moving on to the company’s financial situation. LXFR finished the year with $4.1m in cash and equivalents, as well as 2.2m in restricted cash, which is being held in escrow for the closure of Luxfer Gas Cylinders France. This cash is going to “historic doubtful debt” in the company’s Elektron divisions as well as workers’ compensation insurance. The company also has $42m in long-term debt, which decreased by $25.6m in a year. The company is focusing on reducing its debt, which should help with its debt obligations in the future.

So, is it still worrisome? It’s a lot less worrisome than before. The company finished the year with $30m in operating income while it paid out $5.2m in interest expenses, which means its interest coverage ratio is around 6x and that is well above analysts’ healthy 2x ratio as well as my conservative 5x requirement. Therefore, I don’t see any liquidity or insolvency issues.

Comments on the Outlook

The overarching theme of the last few months has been the tariff war and how unprepared the new Trump Administration is in general. The constant flip-flopping of tariffs or no tariffs is fueling the largest volatility swings for a while now, and even 5% changes in one day in a broad index seems normal. I am interested to see how the company’s operations will be affected by the current tariff situation, which is slightly better than the ones that got delayed by 90 days. However, we shouldn’t forget the fact that there are still tariffs implemented on steel and aluminum as well as a blanket 10% tariff worldwide. This is without a doubt going to affect supply chains globally and increase prices for enterprises, which then will pass on those costs to consumers, leading to higher inflation.

The company has many different facilities around the world that will be affected by tariffs. The company has a facility in Riverside, CA, which relies on imports from Canada of essential inputs like steel and aluminum. These are subject to 25% tariffs and will impact the company’s operations directly by increasing input costs by quite a lot.

The company also has a facility in the UK, which manufactures products for alternative fuel systems and gas cylinders that are exported to the U.S. These exports are facing a 10% blanket tariff, which is going to reduce its competitiveness in the U.S. market.

Maybe the largest problem in terms of operations in the future are the company’s facilities in China. Trump has been punishing China recently and imposed a 125% tariff on all goods while pausing for the other countries (except for the 10% blanket worldwide).

I don’t see how this is not going to be bad for the company in the short run. It depends on international supply chains running smoothly and without barriers like high tariffs. Everything is still up in the year since the new Trump Administration has been known to flip-flop at any time of the day when it comes to what tariffs will be instated, and I expect China to come to some sort of a deal with Trump. I think the U.S. is more dependent on China’s imports than China on the U.S. imports.

I am still very surprised that the company is taking this long to finalize the sale of its Graphics Arts segment. Back in October of last year, I was expecting the business to be divested by Q3. However, we can see that did not pan out, bringing into question the viability of a sale in general. It appears that the buyers are not willing to pay the price LXFR wants, which can only mean the segment isn’t worth as much as LXFR thinks. If it wants to divest it quicker, it needs to lower its sale price, and otherwise it will be stuck with a loss-making business, bringing down its overall operations quite significantly.

What happens if the company isn’t able to divest the business? Well, it means it has to keep putting resources into making it a profitable business, diverting energy from improving what works. That is if it wants to keep the business. Also, if the company doesn’t want to put more resources into a lost cause business, the company could eventually wind the business down, and close operations entirely. This would solve the problem, however, that also means it would have to do some asset write-offs, facility closures, and other costs like severance. It may be able to sell assets individually to at least get some resources out of it.

The management mentioned that they still expect the deal to close in the first half of 2025, however, that is what they said last year too, so we will have to wait and see.

Closing Comments

I am curious to hear what the management has to say about the effect of tariffs on its operations as well as the supposed closing of the sale of its Graphics Arts segment by next quarter. I am not very confident that the operations in general going to continue to improve, and I am expecting some sort of bad news in the upcoming quarter in terms of the outlook for 2025. The company is going to report at the end of this month with analysts expecting the company to make 17 cents on $87m in sales, which points to a 15% sequential decline, as well as around 3% decline y/y.

Therefore, I maintain my hold rating until we get more information on its operations improving further and the sale of the Graphics Arts segment, which has been weighing on its profitability.

data.title: RGC Resources: Worth Watching For A Good Entry Point
data.description:
RGC Resources, Inc. (NASDAQ:RGCO) is a regulated natural gas company serving the greater Roanoke Valley in Virginia. This actually makes the company one of the smallest publicly traded utilities around, as the population of Roanoke is only about 95,400 people. Naturally, though, some of these individuals are children, spouses, or other individuals who do not live alone and so do not need to have their own utility accounts. For its part, RGC Resources claims that it serves about 63,400 customers in its most recent quarterly filing. This is obviously a very far cry from the approximately 2.3 million served by Southwest Gas (SWX) or ONE Gas (OGS) that we discussed earlier this week.

As might be expected, the company’s low relative customer count naturally means that it is a smaller company than the peer firms that we discussed in recent days. As of the time of writing, the company only has a market capitalization of $231.17 million and an enterprise value of $384.86 million. As shown here, this is substantially less than many other natural gas utilities:

Northwest Natural Holding (NWN)

$3.54 billion

New Jersey Resources (NJR)

$4.76 billion

$8.34 billion

As we can see, even the smallest of these companies is several times larger than RGC Resources. This may discourage some investors from purchasing the company’s shares, but there is no reason to avoid a company just because it is relatively small. RGC Resources is still quite liquid, as more than 10,000 shares trade hands on most days:

As such, this should still be a stock that any investor can get in and out of quickly, and unless that investor is trading tens of thousands of dollars in a single trade, it should not move the price too much. At the current price of $22.45 per share, 10,000 shares is $224,500, which is far larger than most individual investors conduct in a single transaction. With that said, though, this daily volume could be a problem for institutional investors, but it should not be problematic for individuals, especially individuals who can spread their purchases out with small trades over a period of a few days. As such, despite the small size of the company, it should be liquid enough for an average person.

The small size of the company could make it too small for institutional investors, however, and this might have actually given it an advantage in the recent market volatility. This chart shows the stock price of RGC Resources against the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the past month:

As we can see, RGC Resources was not affected much by the crash in the prices of most common stocks in response to President Trump’s tariff announcement. One reason for this is that much of the sell-off was caused by leveraged institutional investors (such as hedge funds) dumping stock indiscriminately. A utility company as small as RGC Resources is likely to have many of its shares in the hands of long-term individual holders, who are less likely to have to dump the stock because of margin calls and are less likely to dump just because of a short-term market panic. Thus, RGC Resources might be attractive to a retiree or other long-term holders who are looking to own something relatively safe as a core position in their portfolio.

Speaking of the dividend, RGC Resources is profitable and pays its shareholders a very respectable dividend. As of the time of writing, the yield of the company’s stock is 3.70%, which is substantially higher than the yield of any of the major domestic equity indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index (SPY)

1.31%

NASDAQ 100 Index (QQQ)

0.64%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

1.30%

Some readers might note that these yields are a lot lower than the figures that I provided in an article yesterday. This stands as evidence of how powerfully the market rallied, as it was sufficient to reduce the yields of the major indices by close to ten basis points. In any case, we can see that the yield of RGC Resources is far lower than that of the major market indices, which should be somewhat attractive to anyone who is seeking to earn some income off of their portfolios.

The current yield of RGC Resources is competitive with its peers, but the company is not the highest-yielding one in the space. Here is how RGC Resources compares with its peers:

Company Name

Current Yield

RGC Resources

3.70%

Southwest Gas Holdings

3.53%

ONE Gas

3.65%

Atmos Energy

2.34%

NiSource

2.94%

Northwest Natural Holding

4.85%

New Jersey Resources

3.79%

As we can see here, the current yield of RGC Resources is actually lower than that of New Jersey Resources and particularly Northwest Natural Holding. It is slightly higher than most of the others, however. This might suggest that the market feels that RGC Resources has greater growth potential than Northwest Natural Holding, since it will need to deliver a higher level of capital appreciation in order to deliver a yield that is competitive with its peer. However, it might also be the case that the market is overvaluing RGC Resources for some reason. This is something that we should investigate further.

Recent Performance

I last discussed RGC Resources on November 8, 2023, so about seventeen months have passed since that time. The company's stock performance since that time has been quite good, as it has gained 39.01%, beating the U.S. Utilities Index (IDU) and the S&P 500 Index:

However, we can see that it did appear to have a bit more volatility than the indices, which might be due to the fact that this company has a substantially lower volume than the average large-cap stocks that comprise both indices. This recent volatility might not be what some investors want to see, especially risk-averse retirees and others who are looking at utility stocks, but the outperformance versus the indices is certainly appreciated.

About RGC Resources

As mentioned in the introduction, RGC Resources is a relatively small regulated natural gas utility that serves customers in the greater Roanoke Valley. This is a region of Southwest Virginia that borders the Roanoke River, which would also be considered part of Appalachia:

The company, curiously, does not include a map of its service territory on its website or in any of its investor presentations. The map above shows the region that the Roanoke Valley Association of Realtors considers to be the greater Roanoke Valley region, but this does not necessarily mean that RGC Resources’ infrastructure actually covers this entire five-county area. In fact, it almost certainly does not, given the company’s customer count. However, since the company does not provide a map itself, this is the best information that we have available to us. The company’s service territory is located within the five-county region shown above, particularly the area of Roanoke City and Roanoke County.

The company’s most recent 10-Q filing (linked earlier) states that it has a customer count of 63,400, which is actually a bit less than the 95,400 people who live in Roanoke City alone. This is not particularly surprising, though, considering that a household with multiple people in it will not have multiple natural gas utility accounts. This is the highest figure that the company has ever had in terms of customer count:

This is interesting considering that the city of Roanoke itself is suffering from a population decline. According to the World Population Review, the city’s population is currently declining at a 0.94% rate. The cumulative population decline since the 2020 census is estimated at 4.54%. This is not expected to change anytime soon. As we can see here, the city’s population is expected to have declined to approximately 91,700 people by 2029 compared to 95,400 people today:

This could certainly create headwinds on the company’s growth, although it has done fairly well at sustaining its customer growth so far. Normally, the population decline would be problematic since anyone who leaves the company’s service territory, for whatever reason, can obviously no longer be a customer of it. However, there are two things that we need to keep in mind when looking at this chart. The first is that the company explicitly states that it serves the greater Roanoke Valley, although it does not specifically state which parts of the region it serves. The population projection above is for the city of Roanoke itself, which is the most important city in the region, but not the entire region. If some of the population decline that we see is caused by people leaving the city and settling in the suburbs, they might not be leaving the company’s service territory entirely. A second consideration is that when an individual moves out of a shared household and gets their own place, it can actually provide a boost to the company’s customer count (since an additional household is being served) even in the face of a declining population. Thus, the declining population might not be as big of a headwind as it would at first appear.

There actually could be some evidence that individuals are leaving Roanoke City but remaining within the county. The World Population Review states that Roanoke County is growing its population at a 0.28% growth rate and is expected to continue to grow going forward:

The World Population Review puts the current population of the county at 97,556 people, which is surprisingly not much more than the city itself. The population of Roanoke County is expected to reach 98,600 by 2029. This is still not a high number, especially not when compared to many of the other utilities that we have discussed in this column, but at least it is some growth.

With that said, the population of Roanoke City is expected to decline by 3,700 people through 2029. The population growth of Roanoke County will not fully offset that, as the county is expected to only add approximately 1,044 people over the same period. As such, the demographics of the region could very well be a headwind here.

While the forward demographics of Roanoke City and Roanoke County overall do not look too promising, RGC Resources states in its most recent investor presentation that it has been making an effort to expand its operations into nearby Franklin County. Franklin County is visible on the map above and is located to the south and southeast of Roanoke. Franklin County has very positive demographics, as it also has a growing population. The current population of the county is estimated at 56,489 people, and it is growing at a 0.84% annual rate. This growth is likewise expected to continue:

This estimate puts the expected population of Franklin County at approximately 58,400 people by 2029, an increase of 1,911 people over today’s levels. However, this could be an even bigger growth opportunity for RGC Resources than this figure suggests. This is because the company’s presence in this area is very limited right now. As such, RGC Resources appears to have an opportunity here to connect people to its system who formerly had no natural gas service at all. As such, the company’s opportunity for customer growth in Franklin County is far more than what the population growth alone suggests. Some of the company’s management’s comments suggest that they see this as one of its biggest near-term growth opportunities, and indeed that may be correct.

There is a great deal of evidence that suggests that potential customers are willing to switch over to natural gas whenever it becomes available in their areas. The U.S. Department of Energy reports that in most cases, it is significantly cheaper to heat a home with natural gas than with any other commonly used heating fuel. This fact has certainly not been lost on homeowners, as the Department of Energy reports that one customer every minute connects their property to the infrastructure of a natural gas utility:

As such, RGC Resources should have little difficulty connecting new customers in Franklin County to its utility service. As it expands its distribution network to new housing developments (which it is working to achieve) then it is likely to see people signing up for service and increasing its customer count. This provides a solid avenue for growth in the face of the rather weak demographics in Roanoke City and Roanoke County.

Rate Base Expansion

RGC Resources is also working to increase its rate base as another avenue to generate growth. This should allow it to generate some growth through raising prices on its existing customers, and thus allowing it to grow revenue at a faster rate than it adds new customers.

Unlike most utilities, RGC Resources has not provided a multi-year capital spending plan to its investors. Normally, such a multi-year plan would give investors an idea of what the company’s rate-based growth is likely to be going forward. It also gives us an idea of what the company’s financing needs might be, which could be important if it is going to raise capital through the issuance of new equity. For example, peer company ONE Gas is likely to dilute its shareholders because it will be issuing equity capital to finance its rate base growth over the coming years. Naturally, such a move reduces a company’s forward earnings per share growth, so it would be nice to know if RGC Resources is planning this so that we can adjust our return expectations accordingly.

So far, the only capital investment guidance that RGC Resources has provided shareholders is that it intends to invest approximately $21.7 million into its infrastructure in 2025:

This projection includes $2.8 million into the Mountain Valley Pipeline, which is intended to increase the capacity of that system. RGC Resources only owns 0.73% of that pipeline, so obviously this investment is only a fraction of the total amount needed to complete the capacity expansion of that project. However, the remainder of that needed capital investment will be done by the other partners to the project and are not relevant for our discussion of RGC Resources today.

The actual amount that RGC Resources will be investing in its natural gas utility appears to be approximately $18.9 million this year. That is pretty similar to the amount that the company spent in 2024 and down a bit from 2022 and 2023 levels:

The company grew its rate base at an 8.4% compound annual growth rate over the 2010 to 2024 period. As its 2025 projected spending is in line with the 2024 level, we can expect its rate base growth to be slightly lower because the depreciation of its assets will likely be higher in 2025 than in 2024. After all, the company’s depreciation expense increases every year:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

This is completely normal for a utility and nothing that we need to be concerned about. It does mean, however, that the company would actually need to increase its capital spending every year in order to overcome the rising depreciation and still grow its rate base at the same growth rate. As the company’s planned capital spending in 2025 is in line with 2024, rather than increasing, we can expect its rate base growth to be a little lower than in 2024.

Financial Considerations

As is the case with most utilities and other capital-intensive companies, RGC Resources has seen its interest expenses rise over the past few years:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

As clearly shown, the company’s total and net interest expenses came in at $1.8 million for the most recent quarter, which in the case of RGC Resources was the first quarter of its 2025 fiscal year (RGC Resources has an October to September fiscal year). This represents a 63.64% increase over the $1.1 million that the company spent on debt service during the third quarter of 2022 (the three-month period that ended on June 30, 2022). While this is certainly a fairly large increase over an eleven-quarter period, it was not ridiculously out of line with the increases that its peers reported over the same period:

Company Name

Increase in Total Interest Expense Over Period

Increase in Net Interest Expense Over Period

RGC Resources

63.64%

63.64%

Southwest Gas Holdings

38.91%

36.00%

ONE Gas

144.17%

144.17%

Atmos Energy

101.91%

101.91%

NiSource

116.57%

115.02%

Northwest Natural Holding

117.24%

70.69%

New Jersey Resources

58.41%

53.62%

(figures used to perform the calculations were obtained from Seeking Alpha)

We can see that all of these peer companies saw both their net and total interest expenses increase by quite a lot over the period in question. Southwest Gas was the only exception to this rule, as it managed to limit the amount by which its interest expenses rose over the period. In this light, RGC Resources actually did not do too poorly, as its increase in debt servicing costs was lower than four of the six peer companies in the chart. This is a pretty good situation, overall, especially when we consider that rising interest expenses are a drag on earnings growth.

It unfortunately does appear to be the case that RGC Resources’ rising interest expenses have been a drag on earnings growth. In many cases, we do not mind an increase in interest expenses as long as the company’s revenues rise more rapidly. That situation means that the company is taking on debts to fund projects that generate sufficient revenue to support themselves. We do not see that when we look at RGC Resources’ revenue growth over the period shown above:

(figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

As shown, RGC Resources’ total revenues went from $17.3 million to $27.3 million over the eleven-quarter period. That is only a 57.80% increase, which was lower than what the company’s debt service increase came in at. This tells us that the rising interest expenses have been a drag on the company’s ability to grow its net income and earnings per share. It also tells us that the rising interest expenses have been a headwind for the company’s ability to grow its dividend. After all, a greater proportion of the company’s revenues are needed to service its debt than was the case two years ago. This is a very disappointing situation, and it could be problematic for anyone who is seeking a reliable source of income from their assets.

Valuation

Zacks Investment Research states that RGC Resources is currently trading with a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 17.80. This is better than the 20.61 forward price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 Utilities Sector. Here is how it compares to the company’s peer group:

Company Name

Forward P/E Ratio

RGC Resources

17.80

Southwest Gas Holdings

18.13

ONE Gas

16.86

Atmos Energy

20.46

NiSource

19.80

Northwest Natural Holding

14.28

New Jersey Resources

14.95

(all figures from Zacks Investment Research)

These figures suggest that RGC Resources is at best fairly valued at its current share price. It is the median stock shown in this comparison of other natural gas utilities, so it is hard to make a case that it is undervalued at the current price.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant System appears to agree, as it has assigned a C- valuation grade to the stock at the current price:

This, unfortunately, does not compare very well with the company’s peers:

Company Name

Seeking Alpha Quant Valuation Grade

RGC Resources

C-

Southwest Gas Holdings

B-

ONE Gas

C

Atmos Energy

D-

NiSource

D

Northwest Natural Holding

B+

New Jersey Resources

C+

While not the worst-valued company in the peer group, it is obviously very difficult to make a case for RGC Resources being attractively valued compared to its peers. After all, we see four companies that have more attractive valuations using this system to evaluate them.

Overall, RGC Resources is at best fairly valued right now. It is certainly not undervalued and as such, it may make sense for any investor to wait for a more attractive entry point. The company’s stock is down midday on April 10, so it is possible that a more attractive entry point will present itself in the near future if this continues.

Conclusion

In conclusion, RGC Resources is a small and very underfollowed natural gas utility serving Roanoke and the surrounding area in southwestern Virginia. The fact that the company is much smaller than many of the other utilities that we have discussed in the past is not necessarily a problem, though, as a small company can earn positive net income and cash flow just as well as a large one can. RGC Resources proves this, as the company boasts a fairly impressive 3.70% distribution yield and some growth prospects as it expands into another county near Roanoke.

However, it is difficult to make a case for purchasing the company at its current valuation. It does not appear to be undervalued right now, and several of its peers are more attractively priced. If today’s share price weakness continues going forward, though, then it might start to look attractive. As such, investors who are looking for a safe haven to ride out the market carnage might want to start watching it.

data.title: Tesla Rally? Don't Be Fooled - $42 Is In Sight
data.description:
Like most of us who are invested in some way, it’s almost impossible not to be shaking our heads at the amount of market cap wiped away over the last week. And so far, having owned many of the names in this article, I’ve maintained a bullish stance on companies like Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL), Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), META (NASDAQ:META), Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), with the exception of one: Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA).

That’s right. Despite all the breakthroughs, cloud dominance, explosive growth, rallies, and overall market performance of its MAG7 peers, Tesla stands out as a company I used to own, and may never again.

Spoiler: I rate Tesla as a “Strong Sell.” Not because of how I feel about CEO Elon Musk, but because of the company’s current performance and insurmountable risks to its brand.

Quick Overview

Tesla is one of the most popular electric vehicle manufacturers. Under Elon Musk's leadership, this scrappy start-up has become a dominant player in the auto sector.

Its lineup features high-performance electric vehicles – Model 3 sedan, Model Y crossover, premium Model S and X, and the recently launched Cybertruck with an odd-looking but futuristic design.

Beyond EVs, Tesla expanded into renewable energy with the Powerwall home battery and Megapack commercial storage systems, mainly for consumers who generate clean energy on their own. Tesla’s nationwide Supercharger network also makes long-distance electric travel possible across the country.

Tesla’s Recent Financials

Tesla's Q4 2024 financials alone would be enough to justify my sell rating on TSLA. Although everyone in the Magnificent 7 lineup has had their stock prices crushed thanks to U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariffs causing a possible recession, they all reported positive financials – except for Tesla.

Tesla’s top-line stalled, up a measly 2% year-over-year to $25.7 billion. Its operating income also decreased 23% to $1.58 billion on the back of a 9% increase in operating expenses, which ultimately pushed margins down by 204 basis points, YoY.

But that’s not all.

The most alarming data point in Tesla’s financials is its collapsed bottom line – down 71% year-over-year to $2.33 billion, and its EPS missing estimate by 4.83%.

Tesla’s annual metrics paint a similar picture. Its full-year revenue barely increased by 1% year-over-year to $97.7 billion, with operating income falling 20% to $7.01 billion. Its FY bottom line also took a hit – decreasing 53% to $7.01 billion. And for the icing on the cake: EPS came in at $2.04, down from $4.30 – missing market estimates by 2.42%.

I can’t speak for everyone, but in my perspective, Tesla is at a critical inflection point. Its ability to grow while maintaining costs appears to have deteriorated.

Tesla’s Segmental Revenue

Judging Tesla based on its overall revenue might not paint the whole picture. So, I’ve extracted segmental revenue data for a closer look:

Tesla's Automotive segment – practically its bread and butter – suffered a 6% decline year-over-year to $77 billion, and I think it’s frankly alarming for a company valued as a car manufacturer.

Sure, you can argue that its energy storage and services segments were up by 67% and 27% to $10 billion and $10.5 billion respectively, but from where I stand, these segments operate at a much lower margins than the automotive business, which means these growth rates aren’t sufficient to offset the 6% decline from Tesla’s most important segment.

That’s why looking at the total revenue alone is a classic example of revenue growth covering up an underlying weakness.

Tesla’s 52-Week Stock Price Performance

Over the last 52-weeks, Tesla is up 39.93%. Its share price increased notably since Donald Trump’s election win (late 2024), and the surge appears to be from speculations that Elon Musk’s close ties with Trump would benefit Tesla, Inc.

While that 'benefit' is yet to be seen, TSLA stock tumbled approximately 45% YTD. This whiplash-inducing volatility is also the result of the ongoing bickering between Tesla bulls and bears as Wall Street tries to find the correct valuation.

Moreover, Tesla’s decline coincides with macroeconomic issues like DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) rise that punished almost all companies who heavily invested in AI, and ironically, Donald Trump’s tariffs implementation on various regions, which further escalates the war trade against China and pretty much all other countries.

Unsurprisingly, those factors have pressured everyone in the Magnificent 7 lineup, but Tesla’s standout poor financials make me feel more precarious about the company. Sure, we can see the recovery attempts from some as a “buying opportunity,” but the way I see it, recent trading volume looks like an institutional repositioning.

Also, TSLA’s decoupling from its MAG7 peers during this correction says a lot about its future. In my experience, when macro factors hit an entire sector, but only one company reports poor fundamentals, investors tend to punish that outlier more severely, which appears to be the case for Tesla.

5-YR Revenue Magnificent 7 Comparison

Although Tesla’s 5-YR revenue has grown from $25 billion to $97.8 billion, it’s still in the bottom half of the pack. More importantly, Tesla's growth trajectory – started near the bottom alongside Nvidia, but Tesla's revenue movement shows flattening between 2023-2024, while Nvidia's line shows more notable acceleration during the same period.

For a company valued largely on public sentiment and future potential (rather than actual fundamentals), its current position might raise legitimate questions about Tesla's premium valuation compared to peers – who show either larger scale or more explosive growth acceleration.

I think at Tesla's current valuation, it would require hyper-growth and momentum. However, it’s already showing signs of maturing without having achieved the scale of its peers.

Why I Think Tesla is at Risk of Crashing Further

Until now, we know Tesla's had a bad Q4 2024, and an even worse FY 2024. But, from my perspective, the most concerning factor for Tesla is Elon Musk’s involvement through his White House advisory role, which has resulted in backlashes against the brand.

Several reports have come up about protesters targeting Tesla showrooms, which means not only damages to property (which is likely insured) but potentially long-lasting damage to the brand. If 10, 20, 30%, or more of Tesla’s buyers no longer care about the brand, revenues and profits will crumble even further.

On top of these troubles, Trump's aggressive tariff policies threaten Tesla's supply chain. The company has been reliant on Chinese and Mexican manufacturing components, which means Tesla will have to squeeze its margins just as competition intensifies.

Tesla's declining EV sales would normally mean one of two things: market saturation or diminishing consumer interest. However, neither scenario is the case.

Deliveries Are Down

Customer deliveries in Q1 2025 are down 13% YoY. All this while reports of Tesla's being set on fire as a result of the recent backlash.

This, while Elon Musk continues his pattern of “overpromising.” He claims that his Tesla Robotaxi will start operating in Austin in June, but personally, this is likely unrealistic, given the company’s history of missing deadlines.

Moreover, Elon’s casual suggestion that his current stock price represents "a buying opportunity" seems disconnected from the realities the company is facing – right now.

Another factor that threatens Tesla is BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF). For starters, BYD was once a small Chinese battery manufacturer but is now Tesla’s global competitor.

BYD's FY 2024 revenue reached over $107 billion without selling a single car in the good ol’ US of A – already outpacing Tesla's $97.7 billion. What’s more interesting is that BYD sold significantly more units compared to Tesla. BYD had 4.27 million units sold (EVs and hybrids) while Tesla only had 1.79 million units, which actually declined 1.1% year-over-year for the first time.

Although nothing is certain, BYD’s rise could be the start of a shift in the EV market – outside the U.S.

Tesla’s Valuation Vs. MAG7 Peers

Tesla's forward P/E 97.16x represents a staggering premium compared to its Magnificent 7 peers, especially with its current “growth” rates.

Established tech giants like Microsoft, Meta, Apple, and Amazon trade between 17-27x forward earnings, and even Nvidia – despite the AI boom – trades at just 21.84x. Tesla, however, has multiple nearly four times higher than the S&P 500 Technology sector average (23.77x).

Tesla Stock: My Target Price

In my book, Tesla’s valuation is disconnected from reality. It raises red flags about investor expectations versus Tesla's actual business fundamentals – as we’ve seen from its recent financials.

Tesla is currently priced based on sentiments and what might happen in the future – it's as if Tesla’s Robotaxi and AI ambitions are guaranteed success – while its core business actually struggles due to various factors.

Further, Tesla is confronting multiple headwinds – political backlash tied to Musk’s supply chain issues, tariff policies, and trade wars – yet there’s always someone on one side of the trade, paying a premium that assumes flawless growth from the company.

I don’t know about you, but the growth path I see from Tesla looks nowhere near “flawless growth.”

Bottom line? Barring any major changes to consumer sentiment (i.e., a ton of new orders), my target on TSLA stock is $42 – a 17x multiple – at the low end for a tech company, and at a high end for a vehicle maker, which I think even that might be a bit of a stretch.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Optimism Bumps Up
data.description:
Optimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, neutral sentiment and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 6.8 percentage points to 28.5%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the 13th time in 15 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 3.8 percentage points to 12.5%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 38th time in 40 weeks. Neutral sentiment was last lower on May 28, 2009, (11.0%) and was the 14th-lowest reading in the survey’s history.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 3.0 percentage points to 58.9%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 19th time in 21 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 9.7 percentage points to –30.4%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 14th time in 16 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they would describe the current valuation of stocks.

Here is how they responded:

  • Stocks, in general, are overvalued: 29.2%.
  • Stocks, in general, are fairly valued: 13.0%.
  • Valuations are mixed, with some stocks expensive and others cheap: 36.4%.
  • Stocks, in general, are undervalued: 14.5%.
  • Not sure/no opinion: 6.9%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 28.5%, up 6.8 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 12.5%, down 3.8 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 58.9%, down 3.0 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.
